item.title: Lambda Calculus, Combinatory Logic, and Type Theory
item.description: the lambda calculus something that i have noticed about the lambda calculus is the fact that it is very simple given a programming background; but is full of strange and mysterious names for simple concepts that we programmers see in everyday life. i create these notes as a reference to some of the notations and concepts present within the lambda calculus, as well as some supplementary material such as theorems and definitions.
item.content:
the lambda calculus

something that i have noticed about the lambda calculus is the fact that it is very simple given a programming background; but is full of strange and mysterious names for simple concepts that we programmers see in everyday life.

i create these notes as a reference to some of the notations and concepts present within the lambda calculus, as well as some supplementary material such as theorems and definitions.

lambda calculus is a formal system of logic which aims to describe computation. we can prove that the system is indeed turing complete via the church-turing thesis, which states that; any problem which is computable by the lambda calculus.

the relation between the lambda calculus and turing machine can be formally defined via the church turing thesis, which states what any problem which can be effectively calculable in finite time by a turing machine, can be described via the lambda calculus. (i am sort of paraphrasing, so apologies if it is not as correct as the original definition of the thesis).

we know this term simply as turing completeness, and its a trait that is demonstrated within the majority, if not all programming languages shown today. so by definition, the lambda calculus is technically a bare minimum for a programming language. (with the exception of smaller formal systems such as iota/jot/zot).

basic syntax

the basic syntax of the lambda calculus is as follows

<term> -> <variable>
      | λ <variable>. <variable>
      | ( <term> <term> )

the syntax for the system of logic is so incredibly simple, but as we will see later; it holds the computational power equivalent to that of the turing machine (via the church-turing thesis).

disclaimer

before we proceed, something that i should inform you of is the notation that i will be using throughout the rest of the post. i am not going to be strictly sticking with the syntax, and there will be slight deviations as i will mention now.

  • when defining a lambda abstraction, i will not be using the lambda character. instead, i will be using haskell’s style of defining lambda functions, and simply represent the λ character by a backslash \, followed by the variable.
  • i will omit useless braces at various points within my notes if they are not necessary.

currying

as we saw from the previous bnf definition of the syntax of lambda calculus, every application must only contain a single variable. this means that we cannot pass more than one parameter to a function/ abstraction within the lambda calculus.

for example, here is an invalid function abstraction which performs addition.

\xy.(x + y)

lets curry this function and correct it.

\x.(\y.(x + y))

very nice, now we have a valid abstraction.

free and bound variables | closure & combinators

a bound variable is a variable which is declared within the current function scope.

on the other hand, a free variable is a variable which utilizes a variable from a parent scope. within programming, this can be thought of as using a global variable, or a lambda capturing a variable from a parent scope. in this case, the function can be thought of as a closure.

this may seem rather arbitrary, but some rather interesting complications arise as we begin to work with higher order functions. if we can have closures which inherit scope from a parent, what happens when we return this function? the closure is no longer present underneath the parent scope, so does the scope go with the closure, or does it simply cause an error?

within a lexically scoped language, the closure will keep its scope, and it’s free variables. this may differ in some languages, but it remains true in the lambda calculus.

a combinator on the other hand is the complete opposite, in the sense that it does not use any free variable. this is the only requirement of a combinator. the concept of a combinator can be further elaborated on within the study of combinatory logic (not to be confused with combinatorics).

there is going to be a section for combinatory theory at a later point within the blog. the only thing we need to understand at this point is the concept of the combinator/closure, and how they relate to free/bound variables and higher order functions.

beta reduction

the concept of beta reduction is simple. we simply apply a variable to an abstraction, which we can do simply by substituting. this is simply a fancy name for a very very well understood concept within the rudimentary algebras.

here is a small demonstration of the beta reduction.

(\x.x) 3

we can reduce this simply by substituting the redex by 3, which results in 3.

3

now that was a fairly simple example of beta reduction, lets amp this up a bit and write a lambda abstraction which simply performs addition on the natural numbers.

(\x.(\y.(x + y)) 10) 20

now lets apply a beta reduction to this step by step and view the result.

1. (\x.(\y.(x + y)) 10) 20
2. (\y.(20 + y)) 10
3. (20 + 10)
4. 30

now as we can see, beta reduction simply means substituting the variables within the abstractions.

the church-rosser theorem

when applying variables to lambda abstractions, we also need to know the order in which we are reducing/evaluating the abstraction.

the church rosser theorem states that, “when applying reduction rules to terms, the ordering in which the reductions are chosen does not make a difference to the eventual result”. this theorem essentially states that the lambda calculus is confluent.

so if the result of the abstraction is always the same, then why is the evaluation order/strategy significant?

the church rosser theorem states that all abstractions will always evaluate to the same result regardless of the strategy, but something to note is the fact that not all evaluation strategies will result in a final outcome.

so the theorem states that there is no logical fallacy within the lambda calculus, as it is confluent and will produce the same result after evaluation, but not all evaluation strategies will produce a correct result.

this is where the importance of reduction strategies lie, as we must use the correct order if we want to produce a correct final result.

here is a diagram of the proof for the theorem. this is much easier to visualize as opposed to the textual proof.

this proof of the church rosser theorem directly translates to, “if there are two distinct reductions or sequences of reductions that can be applied to the same term, then there exists a term that is reachable from both results, by applying (possibly empty) sequences of additional reductions”.

beta normal form

beta equivalence

alpha conversion

alpha equivalence

eta conversion

eta reduction

encoding types

untyped lambda calculus by itself is, as the name suggests, inherently untyped. this means that there are no natural numbers, booleans, strings; no types of any kind. however, we can encode types within the untyped formal system, which we can then use to perform calculations and logic.

within the previous demonstrations, i made extra effort in ensuring that i have explicitly specified that the lambda absractions that we were working with were made under the assumption of natural numbers.

church encoding

church numerals

church booleans

church predicates

church pairs

church lists

church encoding demonstration

i find the concept of encoding types within an untyped language particularly interesting, so i have decided to further demonstrate this concept by encoding each monomorphic church type within haskell; along with their associated operations.

scott encoding

scott encoding works under the same concept as the church encoding (which was previously demonstrated).

peano numbers

the fixed point theorem

a fixed point of a function is a value that is mapped to itself by the function. in lambda calculus, the same concept applies in the sense that we can apply the same abstraction by itself, which ultimitely results in something known as the fixed point combinator. the fixed point combinator, also known as the Y combinator; is a means of defining recursion within the lambda calculus.

the Y combinator can be effectively defined as follows.

bohm’s theorem

quasi leftmost reduction theorem

recursion and computation

undecidability

combinatory calculi

combinatory calculi is a branch of mathematical logic which is rooted in the lambda calculus, but extends the system by eliminating the need for free variables. instead, it relies completely on the composition of a finite set of primitive combinatory terms. as we have previously defined, a combinatory term is simply a function that contains no free variables.

de brujin indices

ski combinatory calculus

ibcs combinatory calculus

y combinator

simple typing

subtyping

recursive typing

system F and polymorphism

godels incompleteness theorem

the hindley milner type system

hindley milner type inference

now we are treading into the territory of compiler frontend development with the type inference algorithm of a hindley milner type system. this is an algorithm that it utilizes within a large sum of languages, most notable haskell. this is only required within a statically typed language, which is a language which requires that all types are infered and declared at compile time, so they do not cause problems at runtime.

there are always tradeoffs with every decision within all fields of sciences, so i will attempt to clarify the definitions of programming type systems, as well as the benefits that are inherit within each.

higher order type systems

the lambda cube

static typing

the concept of static typing, as previously stated, is the act of the compiler inferring and handling the type checking at compile time; in the case of programming languages, the frontend compiler.

language such haskell, C, C++, java, rust and more use this; and is commonly seen within languages which compile to machine code, as opposed to some intermediate representation which acts as bytecode for some abstract runtime environment.

the following is a list of the benefits that i can currently think of for statically typed languages.

  • faster runtime spees as the environment does not have to worry about type checking.
    • this remains true for both interpreted and compiled languages.
  • a must for compiling to machine code, as hardware does not support type checking.
  • it makes bugs easy to catch early on, as the compiler can warn the user and terminate compilation.
  • there are probably much more, but my brain is fuzzy right now. sorry uwu.

dynamic typing

a dynamically typed language is a language which performs type checking at runtime. this approach to type checking is only present within interpreted languages, since as previously stated; hardware has no support for type checking within its runtime. it only understand data and code, information and opcodes.

now before we get into the pros of dynamic typing, i want to first explain an extremely important concept within dynamic typing that almost all runtime environment use; and that is call ducktyping.

the concept of ducktyping is very simple. there is a funny little joke that is used that encapsulates this concept fairly well, and serves as the origin of the name: “if it walks like a duck, and quacks like a duck, then it must be a duck”.

this may seem a bit strange and arbitrarily random at first glance, but i will attempt to explain the meaning behind this statement. lets first start off with an example.

within python, everything is stored as an object at runtime. these include literally everything, from integers, lists, strings, functions, classes, modules; everything that is a tangible entity at runtime will be stored as an object. these objects themselves hold their own type information. this allows the object’s type to be changed at runtime dynamically simply by assigning it a different type and values.

when the python runtime want to evaluate something as simple as an expression, it will first need to ensure that the types within the expression are capable of arithmetic. it does do this by categorizing every single type into what it can and cant do, that would be way too inefficient for everybodies standards.

instead, it will simply check to see if the object is capable of the arithmetic operation. it doesnt care about its type at all, it simply check if the object is capable of the operation it wants to do. if the object looks like an integer that can perform arithmetic, then the interpreter will attempt to perform arithemtic on the object.

this drastically speeds up the the type checking, and by product the runtime speeds. i will explain the entire process of the type checking system at a later section within the post, if you are interested then you can skip there directly. there are no additional prerequisite materials for that section of the post besides a fairly general understanding of the c programming language.

the benefits of dynamically typed languages are as shown.

  • lack of type checking pass makes compilation speeds faster.
    • this is generally a massive benefit for scripting languages such as python and lua.
  • dynamic types allow for much faster object oriented runtime polymorphism.
  • generic programming does not have to be restricted at compile time.

strongly typed

now we move onto the concept of strongly and weakly typed systems. something that is very important to note is the fact that these two have NO RELATION TO STATIC/DYNAMIC TYPING!!!! this is something that had confused me when i had originally come across these concepts.

the concept of strongly typed languages simply means that the language itself enforces a restriction on converting/performing operations between types. in this case, it simply means that an integer cannot perform an operation with a float if they are defined as two different types. languages such as haskell and C++ are strongly typed.

this may seem like a very odd and restrictive decision to emplace on a type system for a programming language, but it does come with it’s benefits and solutions.

for example, strongly typed languages will generally include something within their langauge known as ad-hoc or bounded polymorphism. this is when the user may explicitly state what function can operate on which types. i understand that this definition of ad-hoc polymorphism may be a bit vague, but i will do my best to explain this to the best of my capacity.

as you may know, C++ is not exactly the best example to show strong typing, as its foundations are built entirely off of the C programming language, which contains a concept known as implicit conversion. this allows for explicit type casting, and automatic conversions within expressions for a much more streamlined programming experience. for this reason, i will be using haskell to explain this concept.

haskell is a purely functional, statically and strongly typed language which is largely rooted in mathematics. this makes it a very beautiful language, but also a pain to code in. some people may disagree with this statement, but i believe that it is an irrefutable fact that there are better languages that are specifically tailored to the programming experience; as opposed to the purity of the language itself. an example of this is ocaml, but that another rant that i dont want to get into here.

within haskell, there is a concept known as a typeclass. this is a feature that allows you to define a finite set of methods that can operate across multiple types. if you don’t have a background in any functional language, i would highly recommend learning one. it will greatly aid in your understanding of the concepts present within the lambda calculus, which functional programming is largely based on. (i say largely, as some functional languages are not pure).

for those who have experience in C++, ad-hoc/bounded polymorphism is the same thing as operator overloading. operator overloading allows the user to overload functionality of methods/functions on specific types. this is an important staple within generic programming in C++.

the following is a list of the benefits of a strongly typed system.

  • type correctness within the program
    • if the programmer is forced to explicitly defined the set of operations which are allowed on a set of types, then the programmer will know that the expressions are indeed using the correct types; as opposed to allowing the compiler to perform implicit type casts.
  • static analysis is much more useful within a strongly typed languages as the analyzer knows each type of each operand within an expression.
  • eliminates an entire class of bugs/vulnerabilities that are present within weakly typed language, such as type confusion and insecurity.

weakly typed

a weakly typed language is the opposite of a strongly typed language (wow really?), in the sense that the language does not enforce operations between exact types; and may allow for explicit conversion between types.

an example of a weakly typed language is the C programming language (semi-weakly typed). i chose C for this example, as an extremely well known concept within the language is type casting and implicit conversion (as we covered previously). this allows the programmer to explicitly cast the types present within an expression to satisfy the compiler.

this approach places much more freedom within the hands of the programmer and reduces the amount of code they have to write for semi-generic code, but also introduces a class of vulnerability into the language. if used incorrectly, the type safety of the program may potentially be compromised, and may cause undefined behaviour in the best case, and an exploitable bug in the worst.

however, weakly typed languages are not without benefits, as with all tradeoffs. the following is a list of the benefits of weak typing.

  • places much more control in the hands of the developer and allows them to explicitly convert types.
    • this largely depends on the language specification and compiler.
  • reduces code bloat and boiler-plate.
    • the user does not have to explicitly define the operations to be performed on each type to achieve ad-hoc/bounded polymorphism

python’s type checking system

conclusion

thats it for my notes. if youve made it this far then i hope you managed to get something out of this clusterfuck of information. i tried my best to properly format and structure these notes whilst i was taking them, so hopefully they were semi-readable.

if theres any information that was incorrect within these notes, please do me a favour and inform me via one of my contacts listed in my about page. i am always willing to learn more, and if i have misunderstood a concept throughout my process of self study, it would be a massive benefit to be corrected of my misconception.

thanks again for reading. have a great day.

resources

additional resources and references.

lambda calculus

  • https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/lambda-calculus/
  • https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lambda_calculus
  • https://math.stackexchange.com/questions/65622/whats-the-point-of-eta-conversion-in-lambda-calculus
  • https://stackoverflow.com/questions/55996764/lambda-eta-conversion-expansion-reduction-clarification
  • https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Church%E2%80%93Rosser_theorem
  • https://stackoverflow.com/questions/33509954/explaining-the-church-rosser-theorem-in-basic-terms
  • https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Church_encoding

combinatory logic

  • https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Combinatory_logic
  • https://www.amazon.com/Lambda-Calculus-Combinators-Introduction-Roger-Hindley/dp/0521898854
  • https://encyclopediaofmath.org/wiki/Combinatory_logic
  • https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SKI_combinator_calculus
  • https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/B,_C,_K,_W_system
  • https://people.cs.uchicago.edu/~odonnell/Teacher/Lectures/Formal_Organization_of_Knowledge/Examples/combinator_calculus/
  • https://doc.lagout.org/science/0_Computer%20Science/2_Algorithms/Combinatory%20Logic%20in%20Programming.pdf

type theory (not homotopy type theory (HoTT) )

  • https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lambda_cube
  • https://users.cs.northwestern.edu/~jesse/course/type-systems-wi18/type-notes/index.html
  • http://www.mathemafrica.org/?p=13035
  • https://www.amazon.com/Types-Programming-Languages-MIT-Press/dp/0262162091

item.title: Glibc 2.27 - 2.33 Tcache
item.description: Explanation, Implementation, and Exploitation of Tcache What is the Tcache, what purpose does it serve, and how can we exploit it?? To answer these questions, I will be going in detail on the following topics: Tcache internals, glibc 2.27 - 2.33 Source code analysis, and the tcache’s implementation across glibc 2.27 - 2.33 Exploitation techniques across glibc 2.27 - 2.33 The reason I will be creating a new post for glibc versions 2.
item.content:
Explanation, Implementation, and Exploitation of Tcache

What is the Tcache, what purpose does it serve, and how can we exploit it??

To answer these questions, I will be going in detail on the following topics:

  1. Tcache internals, glibc 2.27 - 2.33
  2. Source code analysis, and the tcache’s implementation across glibc 2.27 - 2.33
  3. Exploitation techniques across glibc 2.27 - 2.33

The reason I will be creating a new post for glibc versions 2.27 and 2.33, is that the implementation and function of the tcache will differ across each version. They have slight variations, like how 2.27 will prioritize the fastbin over the tcache, and how 2.33’s tcache has essentially become the sole caching mechanism for all. The other bins still serve a purpose in the optimization of our ptmalloc2 dynamic memory allocator, though not much as I will explain later.

Glibc 2.27

Alright, lets first start of with an explanation of what the tcache is. Tcache, also known as “Thead Local Cache” is a caching mechanism introduced in the release of glibc 2.26. One drawback of the dynamic memory allocator known as dlmalloc was massive overhead when utilized with POSIX threads.

Thus, the release of ptmalloc/Posix Thread(aware) malloc had come prepared with several caching mechanisms that had reduced the overhead caused by pthreads. As great as this caching layer for threading is, the initial introduction had brought along several security vulnerabilities along with it.

Tcache will cache chunks between 24 and 1032 bytes in usable size, which is pretty massive in terms of memory. The usage of the tcache differs between 2.27 and 2.33 as well, and the scenarios in which it will be chosen to cache freed chunks. I will be explaining this within the explanation section, so lets get on with it :)

Explanation 2.27

Lets take a look at the structures that define the tcache within glibc 2.27

#define TCACHE_MAX_BINS 64

typedef struct tcache_entry {
  struct tcache_entry *next;
} tcache_entry;

typedef struct tcache_perthread_struct {
  char counts[TCACHE_MAX_BINS];
  tcache_entry *entries[TCACHE_MAX_BINS];
} tcache_perthread_struct;

Something important that should be noted, is that there is a NEW tcache, for each thread. As we can see by the name, “tcache_perthread_struct”, 1 tcache, per thread. There will be 64 tcache bins per main tcache table, and 7 entries per bin. I will explain this thoroughly here, so its okay to be a bit confused, as this should hopefully all make sense in a bit.

The tcache is a structure that will contain an array of bins. These bins contain the tcache entries, which will essentially be nodes that connect the singly linked list of freed chunks together. Saying this in words will not really aid in the understanding of the tcache without prior knowledge, so lets talk about chunk metadata.

My reasoning behind first explaining metadata, is the fact that everything will be built through chunk metadata. This includes the caching of chunks within bins.

When we are to free a chunk, that does not ACTUALLY become deallocated, the program will simply acknowledge the fact that you have no use for that chunk anymore. Lets take a look at what the heap really is, and explain what and why is making it look so damn complicated.

EXAMPLE HEAP:

0x55555555f000
     |              0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0
     |              0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0
     |              0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0
     |              0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0
     |              0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0
     |              0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0 0x21000
     |              0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0
     |              0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0
     |              0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0
     |              0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0
     |              0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0

     - - - - - - - SNIP - - - - - - -

     |              0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0 0x0
     V
0x555555580000

This is an example of what the heap looks like, I did not worry about the specifics in how the heap will grow upwards, or in how these addresses are not accurate. This is simply a little way to visualize the heap within virtual memory.

As we can see, the little “0x21000” is the wilderness, in which we will discuss later. The important thing to note is that the heap is not some spoopy scary convoluted and intricate mess of chunks flying through memory, it is simply a massive block of free, global, and dynamic space for our program to use.

So what the hell is the tcache, what are chunks? What happens when I allocate memory, and what happens when i free it? how does any of this work, why am i screaming, god is dead!!!

I felt the same way when attempting to learn this, dont worry if you are confused. Everything takes time, just continue to persist and you will come to understand it.

So, we have already established the fact that the heap is just a massive block of free memory for our program to use correct? What happens when we malloc a small chunk?

lets take a look at the following program:

#include <stdlib.h>
int main() {
    malloc(0);
}

okay, so lets take a look at what the chunk will look like after this allocation:

0x0000000000000000 0x0000000000000021
0x0000000000000000 0x0000000000000000
0x0000000000000000

no need for a debugger, i have memorized what a 24 byte chunk will look like in my head

Okay, what is this, why does it look like that?

We have not written anything to this chunk yet, so the 24 bytes of usable memory are left untouched. We know that the 3 “0x0000000000000000”’s after our strange 0x21 is our usabe chunk size by doing some basic math. This is a 64 bit binary, so each address will contain 8 bytes worth of space, and we have 24 usable bytes of space correct?

So 24 / 8 will equals to 3.

That will be our space that we may write to, that will be the pointer that malloc returns. Then what is that weird 0x21? And what is that strange 0x21 before it? That will be the chunk metadata, which contains the necessary information for the entire ptmalloc dynamic memory ecosystem to operate.

Heres an interesting question, how many bytes does free() know how to free? How does it know the chunk size?

Your choices are:

  1. It fetches the sizeof() the pointer we pass to it
  2. It will count the amount of bytes written to the address
  3. It will become self aware of it’s own dynamic memory, and free it however it sees fit

have you made your choice yet?

okaay, 3.. 2… 1……

bzzt! Its none of them!

Let me go through and explain why each of these will not work, lets start with number one:

“It fetches the sizeof() the pointer we pass to it”

This is incorrect, as sizeof() will find the size of the pointer we had passed to it, not the size of the chunk. This is how sizeof works, it will not take the time and effort to go dereference whatever the pointer is pointing to, it will simply return the size of the pointer we passed.

If we allocated an int* pointer to our malloc chunk, it will return the sizeof a pointer, which is 8 bytes on a 64 bit system. So number 1 was not the correct answer, lets take a look at number 2:

“It will count the amount of bytes written to the address”

The reasoning behind why this will be unsuccessful comes with a plethora of reasons. Some of these include the fact that this will cause a massive case of chunk misalignemnt, everything will break, everyone will die.

Another reason why this would not work is that it would cause a massive amount of overhead, which is the main reason why ptmalloc was created in the first place. The goal of dynamic memory is to provide (relatively) fast dynamic memory to the program whenever needed.

The third reason is self explanatory :/

Okay, so you’ve explained all the wrong reasons, what about the right one?

free() will know the chunk size to “free” from chunk metadata. What is metadata you might be thinking, and let me explain this in terms that helped me personally.

This is very similar to photo metadata, in which each file format will contain information about the photo taken. This may be an extremely vague example that introduces more questions than answers for some people that lack prior knowledge, but it is the best example I can provide.

Here is the code that will define our chunk metadata:

struct malloc_chunk {

  INTERNAL_SIZE_T      mchunk_prev_size;  /* Size of previous chunk (if free).  */

  INTERNAL_SIZE_T      mchunk_size;       /* Size in bytes, including overhead. */

  struct malloc_chunk* fd;                /* double links -- used only if free. */
  struct malloc_chunk* bk;


/* ----------------------------------------------------------------------------- */
  /* Only used for large blocks: pointer to next larger size.  */
  struct malloc_chunk* fd_nextsize; /* double links -- used only if free. */
  struct malloc_chunk* bk_nextsize;

};

as we can see from this script, the only piece of metadata that will exist on an allocated chunk is the mchunk_size field, which will contain the size of the current chunk. Lets take a look back at our chunk metadata that was left when we allocated 24 bytes of usable space.

In 64 bit systems, a chunk’s real size will equal to 32 bytes, including the space left for the metadata. so lets check it, 0x21 in decimal is 33, 32 bytes if the usable chunk + metadata, and the extra byte is a flag for the chunk.

These flags consists of the following:

0x1: PREV_INUSE:

This flag will be set if the previous chunk is in use.

0x2: IS_MMAPPED

This flag will be set if the chunk has been mmaped into memory rather than taken a piece off of the heap. This means that free will munmap() the chunk, as opposed to caching it within a freelist bin.

0x4: NON_MAIN_ARENA

This flag will be set if the chunk is NOT in the main arena. We have not talked about this but we will soon, as it is also extremely important. The arena was introduced with the release of ptmalloc2, and it serves as a sort of virtual heap space for each thread. Each thread will have it’s own arena, and if the chunk does not reside within the main arena, this flag will be set.

Okay, we now understand why our chunk’s metadata contained 0x21. It holds the size of the actual chunk, lets take a look at a little visualization of it:

chunk:

[8 - metadata] [1 - flags] [24 - usable chunk]

okay, now we understand the metadata that makes up the chunk, but what about when its freed?

another thing that shoudl be noted is the fact that all caching bins will abide by the rules of the chunk metadata structure, and this applies to the tcache as well.

Lets take a look at a chunk that has been freed:

#include <stdlib.h>
int main() {
    void*ptr=(void*)malloc(0);
    void*ptr2=(void*)malloc(0);
    free(ptr);
    free(ptr2);
    getchar();
}

Lets take a look at what this chunk will look like AFTER it has been freed.

gef➤  x/40gx 0x5555555592c0-64
0x555555559280: 0x0000000000000000      0x0000000000000000
0x555555559290: 0x0000000000000000      0x0000000000000021
0x5555555592a0: 0x0000000555555559      0x0000555555559010
0x5555555592b0: 0x0000000000000000      0x0000000000000021
0x5555555592c0: 0x000055500000c7f9      0x0000555555559010
0x5555555592d0: 0x0000000000000000      0x0000000000000411
0x5555555592e0: 0x0000000000000000      0x0000000000000000
0x5555555592f0: 0x0000000000000000      0x0000000000000000
0x555555559300: 0x0000000000000000      0x0000000000000000
0x555555559310: 0x0000000000000000      0x0000000000000000
0x555555559320: 0x0000000000000000      0x0000000000000000
0x555555559330: 0x0000000000000000      0x0000000000000000
0x555555559340: 0x0000000000000000      0x0000000000000000
0x555555559350: 0x0000000000000000      0x0000000000000000
0x555555559360: 0x0000000000000000      0x0000000000000000
0x555555559370: 0x0000000000000000      0x0000000000000000
0x555555559380: 0x0000000000000000      0x0000000000000000
0x555555559390: 0x0000000000000000      0x0000000000000000
0x5555555593a0: 0x0000000000000000      0x0000000000000000

as we can see, we have our usual 0x21 metadata, but it seems that our usable chunk area has been overwritten with some weird addresses? Why is this happening?

So we already know the structure of the tcache bin, it will hold a counts of the amount of indexs, as well as a pointer to the next free chunk within the tcache, to form a singly linked list.

We notice that there are 2 pointers to the same address @ 0x0000555555559010. What is this doing? Lets check :)

gef➤  x/x 0x0000555555559010
0x555555559010: 0x0000000000000002

okay, so the value at the address is 2? What is this?

This is the count member of our tcache bin, which will hold the count of the chunks within the freelist. As we know, we have allocated and freed 2 chunks. And the other strange address is the tcache next pointer.

Now that we understand what the heap is during allocation, lets visualize what actually happens when we free() a chunk.

First, we malloc a chunk size of 24 and memset the usable chunk area

Nice, so after this operation has been completed, the chunk is officially stored within the tcache bin for later use. Some of these demos may not have been completely accurate, as within glibc 2.27 the free mechanism will prioritize the fastbin over the tcache for a low quantity of frees.

Keep this in mind when working with different glibc versions, always check to see which bin your freed chunk will end up in.

Implementation 2.27

Okay, now that we understand the Thread Local Caching layer for glibc’s ptmalloc, lets begin the source code analysis to reveal any underlying features of the tcache. We want to know how everything will work in tandem with each other, as we still dont know what the code for this while tcache caching layer looks like yet.

Learning the implementation of a certain technology you are attempting to exploit is mandatory in my opinion, so lets get started.

#if USE_TCACHE

typedef struct tcache_entry {
  struct tcache_entry *next;
} tcache_entry;

typedef struct tcache_perthread_struct {
  char counts[TCACHE_MAX_BINS];
  tcache_entry *entries[TCACHE_MAX_BINS];
} tcache_perthread_struct;

static __thread bool tcache_shutting_down = false;
static __thread tcache_perthread_struct *tcache = NULL;

Here we have our usual tcache structures that define everything. We have already gone over this, so I wont be explaining this.

#ifndef INTERNAL_SIZE_T
# define INTERNAL_SIZE_T size_t
#endif

#define SIZE_SZ (sizeof (INTERNAL_SIZE_T))

/* ---- snip ---- */

#define chunk2mem(p)   ((void*)((char*)(p) + 2*SIZE_SZ))
#define mem2chunk(mem) ((mchunkptr)((char*)(mem) - 2*SIZE_SZ))

/* ---- snip ---- */

/* Caller must ensure that we know tc_idx is valid and there's room
   for more chunks.  */

static __always_inline void
tcache_put (mchunkptr chunk, size_t tc_idx) {
  tcache_entry *e = (tcache_entry *) chunk2mem (chunk);
  assert (tc_idx < TCACHE_MAX_BINS);
  e->next = tcache->entries[tc_idx];
  tcache->entries[tc_idx] = e;
  ++(tcache->counts[tc_idx]);
}

Before we get started on the tcache put function, let me first elaborate on these important preprocessor macros as they directly influence our understanding of what the program is doing. Lets first start of with the first definition, of INTERNAL_SIZE_T.

This macro seems to simply hold the size_t data type, which is an unsigned integer capable of holding integer values in range of (0, SIZE_MAX). This seems simple, but there this macro is used often so there is much more that it defines or sets for malloc.

The macro will be used to define the word size for each chunk size. This size will differ between 32 and 64 bit systems, as does each and every integer. We will see this macro a bunch from here on out, so just remember that it will be a 64 bit unsigned integer, on 64 bit systems.

the SIZE_SZ macro is simply the sizeof INTERNAL_SIZE_T, which will be 8 bytes. This will simply hold the size of the INTERNAL_SIZE_T macro, lets move on.

Next, we have our chunk2mem macro function, this will utilize the SIZE_SZ macro, and will be used within the tcache_put function, so lets go over what it will do. As we can see, chunk2mem will take a pointer, dereference it with a char data type, then dereference as a void ptr. This will essentially attempt to first dereference (p + 8) with a char pointer. Then, cast void pointer onto it.

Finally, we have the tcache_put function, it will take 2 parameters for this function. It will take a pointer to the chunk we want to cache within the tcache freelist, and it will contain a tcache index. This does 2 things, it will allow the tcache to sort the chunk by bin, and will also define the size of the chunk.

Since each bin is indexed by size, then if we were to pass the index to what bin we want to cache this chunk in, we now also know the size of the chunk.

Alright, so the function will then allocate a new tcache entry by converting the chunk we had passed to the function into memory. It will then call an assert statement making sure that the index passed to the tcache is still within the range of the max tcache size, which is 64.

Then, it will simply update the data, and insert/append our node/entry into the tcache linked list. This is standard data structures stuff, so I will not be explaining the concept of linked lists in this post but you are free to research creation, insertion, reversal, searching, sorting, and deletion of singly and or doubly linked lists in your free time.

Then, it will properly incrememnt the count of our tcache bin.

/* Caller must ensure that we know tc_idx is valid and there's
   available chunks to remove.  */

static __always_inline void *
tcache_get (size_t tc_idx)
{
  tcache_entry *e = tcache->entries[tc_idx];
  assert (tc_idx < TCACHE_MAX_BINS);
  assert (tcache->entries[tc_idx] > 0);
  tcache->entries[tc_idx] = e->next;
  --(tcache->counts[tc_idx]);
  return (void *) e;
}

Next, we have the tcache_get function, which will retrieve an entry from the linked list, and return a pointer to it. The comment on the top is simply informing us that this function does not have much checking put in place. So the caller of the function will have to make sure that chunk exists within the tcache freelist.

Okay, first it will construct a pointer to the entry that we want to retrieve from the bin. It will then add some assert checks to make sure everything is within bounds, and will then retrieve the entry without destroying the linked list.

Lastly, it will decrement the counts of the tcache, and will return a void pointer to the entry we removed from the tcache freelist. Not so bad, so these two functions will handle the insertion and removal of chunks into and from the tcache.

Next we have the free() source code, I have trimmed out the parts that did not relate to the tcache, and this was the result. Lets walk through this:

static void
_int_free (mstate av, mchunkptr p, int have_lock)
{
  INTERNAL_SIZE_T size;        /* its size */
  mfastbinptr *fb;             /* associated fastbin */
  mchunkptr nextchunk;         /* next contiguous chunk */
  INTERNAL_SIZE_T nextsize;    /* its size */
  int nextinuse;               /* true if nextchunk is used */
  INTERNAL_SIZE_T prevsize;    /* size of previous contiguous chunk */
  mchunkptr bck;               /* misc temp for linking */
  mchunkptr fwd;               /* misc temp for linking */

  size = chunksize (p);

  /* Little security check which won't hurt performance: the
     allocator never wrapps around at the end of the address space.
     Therefore we can exclude some size values which might appear
     here by accident or by "design" from some intruder.  */

  if (__builtin_expect ((uintptr_t) p > (uintptr_t) -size, 0)
      || __builtin_expect (misaligned_chunk (p), 0))
    malloc_printerr ("free(): invalid pointer");

  /* We know that each chunk is at least MINSIZE bytes in size or a
     multiple of MALLOC_ALIGNMENT.  */

  if (__glibc_unlikely (size < MINSIZE || !aligned_OK (size)))
    malloc_printerr ("free(): invalid size");

  check_inuse_chunk(av, p);

#if USE_TCACHE
  {
    size_t tc_idx = csize2tidx (size);

    if (tcache
	&& tc_idx < mp_.tcache_bins
	&& tcache->counts[tc_idx] < mp_.tcache_count)
      {
	tcache_put (p, tc_idx);
	return;
      }
  }
#endif
}
 ---- snip ----

First, free will initialize pointers so it is able to read the heap metadata and work according to what it sees. Everything here, we have talked about when we spoke about the chunk structure, so I will not be going over that.

Next, we have a little security check that attempts to validate whether the pointer passed to free is of valid size and or address.

Next, we get to the important part, which is where the tcache has been implemented within free. Here is where free() will attempt to store chunks within the tcache freelist, so lets walk through this.

first, it will find the size of the chunk, and convert it into an index for the bin. This is due to each bin being indexed by size.

Next, it will check if the tcache exists, and if the tcache bins exist, and if the index’s counts is less than the tcache count. It will essentially place a bunch of checks in place, and then insert the pointer into the index entry into the tcache.

This is the important piece of code within the free function, that concerns the tcache. When a chunk has been freed, it will simply just cache the chunk, if the “USE_TCACHE” is set.

Next, we have our big malloc function, lets walk through this one as well, i have kept only the important pieces of code that pertain to the tcache.

 static void *
 _int_malloc (mstate av, size_t bytes)
 {

 ---- snip ----

 #if USE_TCACHE
   size_t tcache_unsorted_count;     /* count of unsorted chunks processed */
 #endif

 ---- snip ----

 #if USE_TCACHE
           /* While we're here, if we see other chunks of the same size,
              stash them in the tcache.  */
           size_t tc_idx = csize2tidx (nb);
           if (tcache && tc_idx < mp_.tcache_bins)
             {
               mchunkptr tc_victim;

               /* While bin not empty and tcache not full, copy chunks over.  */
               while (tcache->counts[tc_idx] < mp_.tcache_count
                      && (pp = *fb) != NULL)
                 {
                   REMOVE_FB (fb, tc_victim, pp);
                   if (tc_victim != 0)
                     {
                       tcache_put (tc_victim, tc_idx);
                     }
                 }
             }
 #endif

the first piece of code that will mention the tcache will simply initialize the tcache_unsorted_count as an unsigned long. Next, it will use a mechanism in which it will copy the chunks from the fastbin over into the tcache, in reverse order.

The reasoning behind it copying it back in reverse order is not exactly a preference, as much as it is an inherent trait that LIFO singly linkled lists posess. I had talked about this relationship between the tcache and fastbin during my explanation of the fastbin_reverse_into_tcache.

anyways, lets move onto the next snippet of code

 #if USE_TCACHE

   INTERNAL_SIZE_T tcache_nb = 0;
   size_t tc_idx = csize2tidx (nb);
   if (tcache && tc_idx < mp_.tcache_bins)
     tcache_nb = nb;
   int return_cached = 0;

   tcache_unsorted_count = 0;
 #endif

 ---- snip ----

               /* Fill cache first, return to user only if cache fills.
                  We may return one of these chunks later.  */
               if (tcache_nb
                   && tcache->counts[tc_idx] < mp_.tcache_count)
                 {
                   tcache_put (victim, tc_idx);
                   return_cached = 1;
                   continue;
                 }
               else
                 {

This will create a size_t variable called tcache_nb, then convert chunk size into bin index. If the tcache exists, and if the tcache index is less than the total amount of tcache bins, then set the variable tcache_nb to nb. Then also set return_cached to 0, and tcache_unsorted_count to 0.

Then, if the tcache_nb is set, and the counts are not out of bounds, it will then place the victim chunk into the tcache, at the provided index. No, this is not a POC for demoing a new house of exploitation technique, this pointer is actually named victim within the glibc source code.

 #if USE_TCACHE
       /* If we've processed as many chunks as we're allowed while
          filling the cache, return one of the cached ones.  */
       ++tcache_unsorted_count;
       if (return_cached
           && mp_.tcache_unsorted_limit > 0
           && tcache_unsorted_count > mp_.tcache_unsorted_limit)

         }
 #endif
